metadata.subject: Science
metadata.description: Environmental Science 12 examines critical environmental challenges of the 21st century: water quality and security, climate change, sustainable land use, and the pursuit of sustainable living. Students investigate these issues through scientific inquiry, quantitative data analysis, and ethical reasoning — drawing on both Western science and First Peoples ecological knowledge. The course prepares students for environmental science, sustainability, and related post-secondary pathways.
metadata.general_description: An advanced senior environmental science course examining water security, climate change, sustainable land use, and sustainable living — developing scientific inquiry, environmental literacy, and ethical reasoning for informed citizenship and post-secondary pathways.

big_ideas.statement: Human actions affect the quality of water and its ability to sustain life.
big_ideas.elaboration: Water is Earth's most essential resource — yet human activities from agriculture and industry to urbanization and climate change are degrading water quality and availability for both ecosystems and human populations. Understanding these impacts is the foundation of water stewardship.

big_ideas.statement: Human activities cause changes in the global climate system.
big_ideas.elaboration: The scientific evidence that human greenhouse gas emissions are driving climate change is overwhelming and converging. Understanding the mechanisms, feedback loops, and projected impacts of climate change is essential for developing meaningful mitigation and adaptation strategies.

big_ideas.statement: Sustainable land use is essential to meet the needs of a growing population.
big_ideas.elaboration: Land is a finite resource under increasing pressure from food production, urbanization, and resource extraction. Sustainable land management — informed by both science and Indigenous knowledge — is essential to maintain ecosystem services for current and future generations.

big_ideas.statement: Living sustainably supports the well-being of self, community, and Earth.
big_ideas.elaboration: Sustainability is not just an environmental concept — it is a framework for living that integrates ecological limits with human well-being, economic justice, and intergenerational responsibility. First Peoples understandings of reciprocal relationships with the land offer profound guidance.

competencies.raw: Demonstrate sustained intellectual curiosity about scientific topics of personal, local, or global interest; make observations to identify questions; formulate multiple hypotheses and predict outcomes.
i_can_statements: I can identify complex questions about water security, climate change, land use, and sustainability.
I can formulate multiple hypotheses about environmental processes and predict probable outcomes.
I can connect personal observations to local and global environmental questions.

competencies.raw: Collaboratively plan and select appropriate investigation methods; assess risks and ethical issues; use SI units and equipment systematically; apply accuracy and precision concepts.
i_can_statements: I can design environmental investigations — field-based and laboratory — using appropriate methods.
I can assess safety, ethical, and cultural considerations in environmental research.
I can collect accurate environmental data (water quality, soil, air) using appropriate instruments and SI units.

categories.name: Processing and Analyzing Data and Information
categories.raw: Processing and Analyzing Data and Information

competencies.raw: Interpret local environments; apply First Peoples perspectives; analyze patterns and trends; construct and interpret graphs; draw evidence-based conclusions; analyze cause-and-effect relationships.
i_can_statements: I can apply First Peoples ecological knowledge and land stewardship frameworks to environmental analysis.
I can analyze patterns and trends in environmental data — climate records, water quality indices, land use maps.
I can construct and interpret graphs of long-term environmental change, including climate projections.
I can draw evidence-based conclusions about environmental cause-and-effect relationships.

competencies.raw: Assess methods and identify error sources; improve investigation quality; evaluate model validity; recognize bias; connect to science careers; critically analyze information sources.
i_can_statements: I can evaluate the methods and limitations of environmental studies including sampling bias.
I can assess the validity of climate models and environmental projections.
I can recognize political and economic bias in environmental claims and evaluate information sources critically.
I can consider the ethical, social, and economic dimensions of environmental policy decisions.

competencies.raw: Contribute to community care; design projects with local and global connections; solve real-life problems; consider scientists' roles in innovation.
i_can_statements: I can apply environmental science concepts to design a sustainability project with local impact.
I can connect environmental science learning to careers in environmental management, policy, and research.
I can consider Indigenous-led environmental governance as both legitimate and effective.

competencies.raw: Formulate models; communicate scientific ideas with evidence-based arguments; express varied perspectives through place-based learning.
i_can_statements: I can communicate complex environmental issues using appropriate scientific vocabulary and evidence.
I can present evidence-based arguments about environmental policy to a general audience.
I can express how First Peoples perspectives and local landscapes shape our understanding of environmental sustainability.

areas.name: Water Quality and Security
areas.raw: Human actions affecting quality of water and its ability to sustain life.
topics: Water quality parameters: pH, dissolved oxygen, turbidity, nitrates, phosphates, coliform bacteria
Bioindicators: using aquatic macroinvertebrates and other organisms to assess water quality
Sources of water pollution: agricultural runoff, industrial discharge, urban stormwater, mining
Wastewater treatment: primary, secondary, and tertiary treatment processes
Global water security: freshwater scarcity, access inequity, and virtual water
Water governance: water rights, Indigenous water rights, and international water law
BC water issues: salmon habitat, water licences, and the Columbia River Treaty

areas.name: Climate Change
areas.raw: Human activities causing changes in the global climate system.
topics: Earth's energy balance: solar radiation, albedo, and the greenhouse effect
Greenhouse gases: CO₂, CH₄, N₂O, water vapour — sources, sinks, and radiative forcing
Climate feedbacks: ice-albedo feedback, permafrost methane, water vapour amplification
Evidence for climate change: temperature records, ice cores, sea level rise, ocean acidification
Projected impacts: extreme weather, ecosystem disruption, agricultural stress, human displacement
Mitigation strategies: renewable energy, carbon capture, energy efficiency, electrification
Adaptation strategies: sea walls, drought-resistant crops, wildfire management, ecosystem corridors
First Peoples climate knowledge: traditional indicators, climate observations, and adaptation

areas.name: Sustainable Land Use
areas.raw: Sustainable land use essential to meet needs of a growing population.
topics: Soil science: composition, horizons, organic matter, fertility, and erosion
Land use types: agriculture, forestry, urban development, protected areas, and industrial extraction
Land degradation: erosion, desertification, soil salinization, and habitat loss
Sustainable agriculture: regenerative farming, organic practices, cover crops, and no-till
Sustainable forestry: old-growth protection, selective harvesting, and certification
Urban land use: green infrastructure, urban forests, and brownfield remediation
Indigenous land stewardship: territorial management, cultural burning, and land title
Land use policy: agricultural land reserve, zoning, and environmental assessment in BC

areas.name: Living Sustainably
areas.raw: Living sustainably supporting well-being of self, community, and Earth.
topics: Ecological footprint: measuring and reducing personal and community impact
Consumption patterns: food systems, fast fashion, planned obsolescence, and circular economy
Energy choices: household energy use, renewable options, and transportation decisions
Waste reduction: zero waste principles, composting, recycling, and extended producer responsibility
Environmental ethics: anthropocentrism, biocentrism, ecocentrism, and Indigenous relational ethics
Environmental law and policy: key Canadian and BC legislation, environmental assessment
Community action and environmental justice: grassroots movements, activism, and Indigenous-led governance
Sustainability careers and pathways: environmental science, green technology, policy, and education
